content.text: Privacy Nutrition Labels for Claude API Calls

Since December 2020, Apple has required every iOS app on the App Store to declare its data practices through Privacy Nutrition Labels — a structured disclosure visible on the app's product page before anyone downloads it. Adding Claude to your app creates new data flows that these labels must cover. Failing to declare them is one of the top reasons AI apps are rejected during review, and updating declarations post-rejection requires a new build submission cycle.

This document walks through exactly which data types a Claude-powered app must declare, how to determine the correct answers to App Store Connect's questions, how to minimise your disclosure surface through PII-reduction at the code level, and how to write a
PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy
manifest for SDK dependencies.

attrs.alt: Privacy Nutrition Label table showing data types a Claude API app must declare in App Store Connect
attrs.title: Privacy Nutrition Label for a Claude API App

content.text: The Three Questions App Store Connect Asks
For each data type you declare, App Store Connect asks three questions. Getting these right matters — incorrect answers can trigger rejection or, worse, enforcement action after the app ships.
Is this data used to track the user?
Tracking means linking user data to third-party advertising identities or data brokers. Sending a user's prompt to Anthropic's Claude API is
not
tracking — it is app functionality. Answer:
No
for Claude API calls, unless you also share identifiers with an ad network.
Is this data linked to the user's identity?

Data is 'linked' if it is associated with an account, device, or user profile. If you send a prompt alongside a user ID or session token (as you typically do through a backend proxy), the prompt is linked. If you send it anonymously with no session context, it may not be.
What is this data used for?
The allowed purposes include App Functionality, Analytics, Developer's Advertising, Third-Party Advertising, and Product Personalisation. Claude API calls fall under
App Functionality
. If you log which features users invoked, that is also
Analytics
Data Types Required for a Claude App
User Content — the most-missed declaration
Any text the user types and that leaves the device is 'User Content' under Apple's taxonomy. This includes:
Chat messages and prompts entered in a text field
Voice transcriptions that are then sent as text
Pastes from the clipboard that the user triggers

The common mistake is believing that because Anthropic (not your servers) processes the prompt, you don't need to declare it. This is incorrect — from Apple's perspective, your app collected the data and sent it to a third-party service.
You must declare User Content regardless of who ultimately processes it.
Declare:
Data Category → User Content → Other User Content. Purpose → App Functionality. Linked to User → Yes (if user is signed in).
Identifiers

If your backend proxy authenticates requests with a session token, user ID, or device ID (which it should — unauthenticated AI proxies are a rate-abuse target), those identifiers travel with every Claude API call.
Declare:
Data Category → Identifiers → User ID. Purpose → App Functionality (rate limiting, authentication). Linked to User → Yes.
Usage Data

If your app records which AI features a user invoked (e.g., 'summarise', 'translate', 'explain'), how long sessions lasted, or how often Claude was called, that is Usage Data. Even if you only aggregate it on the server, the data leaves the device linked to a user context.
Declare:
Data Category → Usage Data → Product Interaction. Purpose → Analytics. Linked to User → depends on whether you use aggregation only (No) or per-user event logs (Yes).
Diagnostics

Crash logs stay on the device by default (Xcode Organizer / MetricKit pulls them). If you send error reports — including Claude API error codes — to a backend analytics service like Sentry or Datadog, declare Diagnostics.
Declare:
Data Category → Diagnostics → Crash Data. Purpose → App Functionality. Linked to User → No (crash reports do not carry PII).
Reducing Your Declaration Surface with PII Redaction
Every data type you declare adds review scrutiny and user concern. The best strategy is to not send PII to Claude in the first place. A
PIIRedactor

struct strips email addresses and phone numbers from user input before the prompt leaves the device. This means you can honestly declare that Claude API calls do not contain Contact Info, even if users paste contact details into the text field.
With
PIIRedactor.redact(_:)
applied before every prompt is sent, an input like
'Call me at 555-123-4567 or email me at jane@example.com'
becomes
'Call me at [REDACTED_PHONE] or email me at [REDACTED_EMAIL]'

before it reaches Claude. Claude can still respond meaningfully ('I see you provided contact details — I cannot use those but I can help with...') while your app truthfully omits Contact Info from its nutrition label.
Integrating PIIRedactor into the proxy call
Privacy Manifests (PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy)
Starting with Xcode 15 / iOS 17, Apple requires apps and third-party SDKs that access certain APIs (file timestamps, user defaults, disk space, and others) to declare a
PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy
manifest. This is a separate requirement from the nutrition labels in App Store Connect — it is a file bundled into your app that documents API usage at build time.
For a Claude integration that routes through a networking layer, the relevant required-reason APIs are typically
NSUserDefaults
(if you cache conversation history) and file-system APIs (if you persist responses to disk). A minimal
PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy
for such an app looks like:

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<!DOCTYPE plist PUBLIC "-//Apple//DTD PLIST 1.0//EN"
    "http://www.apple.com/DTDs/PropertyList-1.0.dtd">
<plist version="1.0">
<dict>
    <!-- Required-reason APIs your app uses -->
    <key>NSPrivacyAccessedAPITypes</key>
    <array>
        <!-- NSUserDefaults: used to store user preferences and conversation settings -->
        <dict>
            <key>NSPrivacyAccessedAPIType</key>
            <string>NSPrivacyAccessedAPICategoryUserDefaults</string>
            <key>NSPrivacyAccessedAPITypeReasons</key>
            <array>
                <!-- CA92.1: Access user defaults for app preferences -->
                <string>CA92.1</string>
            </array>
        </dict>
    </array>

    <!-- Declare collected data types (mirrors App Store Connect labels) -->
    <key>NSPrivacyCollectedDataTypes</key>
    <array>
        <dict>
            <key>NSPrivacyCollectedDataType</key>
            <string>NSPrivacyCollectedDataTypeOtherUserContent</string>
            <key>NSPrivacyCollectedDataTypeLinked</key>
            <true/>
            <key>NSPrivacyCollectedDataTypeTracking</key>
            <false/>
            <key>NSPrivacyCollectedDataTypePurposes</key>
            <array>
                <string>NSPrivacyCollectedDataTypePurposeAppFunctionality</string>
            </array>
        </dict>
    </array>

    <!-- This app itself does not track users -->
    <key>NSPrivacyTracking</key>
    <false/>
</dict>
</plist>
Add this file to your Xcode project at the root of the app target (not inside a source folder). Xcode validates it at build time and includes it in the app bundle. If a dependency SDK ships its own
PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy
, Xcode merges them automatically.
App Store Connect Step-by-Step
When preparing a new version submission in App Store Connect, navigate to
App Privacy
and complete these sections for a typical Claude app:
Data collection:
Select 'Yes, we collect data from this app.'
Data types:
Check User Content, Identifiers, and Usage Data. Leave Contact Info, Location, Health, and Financial Info unchecked if you applied PII redaction and do not collect those.
Per-type questions (User Content):
Used for tracking: No. Linked to user: Yes (if users log in). Purpose: App Functionality.
Per-type questions (Identifiers):
Used for tracking: No. Linked to user: Yes. Purpose: App Functionality.
Per-type questions (Usage Data):
Used for tracking: No. Linked to user: No (if aggregated). Purpose: Analytics.
Writing a Privacy Policy That Covers Claude
App Store guidelines require a privacy policy URL in App Store Connect. Your policy must mention the third-party AI processor. A minimal disclosure for Claude:

AI Features

[App Name] uses Claude, an AI assistant developed by Anthropic, to power [describe
feature]. When you use these features, the text you enter is transmitted to
Anthropic's servers for processing. Anthropic processes this data in accordance
with their privacy policy at https://www.anthropic.com/legal/privacy.

We do not sell, share for advertising, or retain your AI conversation text beyond
what is necessary to provide the feature. [Modify to match your actual retention.]

You may opt out of AI features at any time by [describe opt-out path].

Apple reviewers may check whether the privacy policy URL in App Store Connect is reachable and whether it covers data flows visible in the app. A policy that does not mention AI processing while the app prominently uses it is a red flag.
Testing Your Declarations with a Data Audit
Before submitting, run a network audit to confirm your declarations match actual traffic:

Run the app through its main AI workflows with this protocol registered, then compare the logged domains against your nutrition label declarations. Every domain that receives user-entered text should correspond to a declared data type.
Common Mistakes and How to Avoid Them
Not declaring User Content because 'Anthropic processes it':
Your app collects and transmits the text — the processor is irrelevant for disclosure purposes. Always declare it.
Checking 'Used for Tracking' for Claude API calls:

Tracking has a specific Apple definition — linking data to third-party advertising. Claude API calls are App Functionality, not tracking. Erroneously checking this box makes your app appear to run ads.
Declaring Contact Info without PII redaction:
If you don't redact emails/phones before sending prompts, you must declare Contact Info. This invites more privacy scrutiny. Redaction is easier than the additional disclosure.
Omitting PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy:

Apps submitted without the manifest when accessing required-reason APIs (NSUserDefaults, file timestamps) receive a rejection notice beginning in Spring 2024. Check Xcode's Privacy Report (Product → Archive → Distribute) to see what Xcode detected.
Nutrition label not matching actual data flows:

Apple's reviewers install the app and can monitor network traffic. If they see requests to Anthropic's servers that don't correspond to a declared data type, the submission is rejected with a mismatch notice.
Summary

For a Claude-powered iOS app, the mandatory nutrition label declarations are User Content (prompts sent to Anthropic), Identifiers (user/device ID sent with proxy requests), and Usage Data (AI feature analytics). Apply
PIIRedactor
before every API call to avoid also declaring Contact Info. Bundle a
PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy

manifest to cover required-reason API access. Verify declarations match actual traffic using a debug URL protocol interceptor before submission. Accurate labels submitted on the first attempt save a full App Store review cycle — typically several days to a week.
